coredata.dc:title: Dual-nanocluster of copper and silver as a ratiometric-based smartphone-assisted visual detection of biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Alshatteri, Azad H.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric-based probes improve detection precision and accuracy by minimizing the influence of various experimental conditions. Here, we present a unique dual-emission fluorescence probe constructed by a dual-nanocluster. This sensing platform is composed of copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) as a reference signal and silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) as a response signal. Uracil was used as a capping agent to improve the stability of AgNCs by forming a complex with silver metal. Ratiometrically, the composite of copper/silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Cu@Ag NCs) was used to measure the amount of cysteine as a typical biothiol. The Cu@Ag NCs showed a reddish fluorescence that originated from AgNCs and CuNCs when excited by a UV lamp. Under UV illumination, the addition of cysteine caused a noticeable fluorescence color shift from red to blue. Due to the color tonality, a smartphone and the Color Grab app were used to analyze red, green, and blue (RGB) values from the images of the dual-nanocluster solutions before and after the addition of cysteine. A satisfactory linear correlation between the ratio of red to blue values as a function of cysteine concentration was established for the quantitative measurement of cysteine from 20 to 180 μM with a limit of detection of 7.0 μM. The proposed probe was applied to assay cysteine in human serum with satisfactory accuracy and precision.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoclusters
Copper nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescent
Smartphone-based detection
RGB Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cys in real sample
Synthesis and characterization of CuNCs, AgNCs and Cu@Ag NCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: For all of the assistance offered, the authors are grateful to the Universities of Sulaimani and Garmian.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of Cu@Ag NCs nanocomposite. (B) FTIR spectra of the synthesized Cu@Ag NCs nanocomposite and BSA-PVP-Uracil. (C) The UV–vis spectra of BSA-PVP-uracil, CuNCs, AgNCs, and Cu@Ag NCs (D) Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs (blue short dot, excited at 325 nm), AgNCs (red short dots, excited at 470 nm), and Cu@Ag NCs (red solid line, excited at 380 nm as the maximum excitation to achieve blue and red peaks).
EDS spectra, (A) AgNCs (insert quantitative chemical composition), (B) CuNCs (insert qualitative chemical composition), (C) and (D) are elemental mapping images for AgNCs and CuNCs, respectively.

(A) using ImageJ for splitting the captured image into its three RGB channels (red, green, blue). (B) The graph between red to blue values as a function of different concentrations of cysteine (insert: the image of the nanocomposite with several cysteine concentrations under UV irradiation), (C) The fluorescence intensity of the Cu@Ag NCs nanocomposite in the presence of biothiol and other interferents. a: ascorbic acid; b: citric acid; c: creatinine; d: l-tyrosine; e: folic acid; f: glucose; g: uric acid; h: histidine; i: l-arginine; k: urea; l: theobromine.
A schematic diagram of the fabrication of Cu@Ag NCs composite and Cys detection.
The mechanism of quenching of AgNCs in the presence of cysteine.
Comparison of smartphone-based detection works used to detect cysteine with current work.
Spiked recovery% for blood serum samples using the Cu@Ag NCs probe. Number of replicates = 3.

head.ce:title: Dual-nanocluster of copper and silver as a ratiometric-based smartphone-assisted visual detection of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St, 46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St, 46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Education, University of Garmian, Darbandikhan Road, 46021, Kalar City-Sulaimaniyah Province, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Education, University of Garmian, Darbandikhan Road, 46021, Kalar City-Sulaimaniyah Province, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biomedical Analysis, Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St, 46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biomedical Analysis, Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St, 46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric-based probes improve detection precision and accuracy by minimizing the influence of various experimental conditions. Here, we present a unique dual-emission fluorescence probe constructed by a dual-nanocluster. This sensing platform is composed of copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) as a reference signal and silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) as a response signal. Uracil was used as a capping agent to improve the stability of AgNCs by forming a complex with silver metal. Ratiometrically, the composite of copper/silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Cu@Ag NCs) was used to measure the amount of cysteine as a typical biothiol. The Cu@Ag NCs showed a reddish fluorescence that originated from AgNCs and CuNCs when excited by a UV lamp. Under UV illumination, the addition of cysteine caused a noticeable fluorescence color shift from red to blue. Due to the color tonality, a smartphone and the Color Grab app were used to analyze red, green, and blue (RGB) values from the images of the dual-nanocluster solutions before and after the addition of cysteine. A satisfactory linear correlation between the ratio of red to blue values as a function of cysteine concentration was established for the quantitative measurement of cysteine from 20 to 180 μM with a limit of detection of 7.0 μM. The proposed probe was applied to assay cysteine in human serum with satisfactory accuracy and precision.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing, which uses two or more fluorescent bands with different wavelengths, is a promising probe, especially for visual detection based on color tonality [1–3]. Most fluorescent probes in traditional fluorescence-based mode only use a single-wavelength emission basis for quenching or enhancing [4]. Because of the absence of an internal standard, the fluorescence signals of these probes can fluctuate depending on the experimental parameters, including variations in the environment of the solution, photobleaching, concentration uncertainty of the probes, and stability of excitation intensity [5]. The fluctuations of the experimental parameters lead to systematic and random errors. These fluctuations indicated above are overcome by the ratiometric design since it offers a built-in adjustment against outside interferences, considerably improving the accuracy and reliability of the detection outcomes [6–9].

Variations in ratiometric fluorescence cause a series of color tonalities that are distinct enough to be seen with the naked eye and are applied to point-of-care diagnostics [6,10]. As a result, developing a ratio-fluorescence approach to increase the sensing performance of a single probe is critical. The traditional ratiometric probes were organic dyes with two distinct fluorescence peaks. However, choosing organic dyes can be challenging since many of them still have drawbacks, such as low fluorescence quantum yield, weak photobleaching resistance, narrow excitation and broad emission bands, and particularly the difficulties of organic molecule synthesis and purification [11]. Many other fluorescence emitters have been used either as single emission or ratiometric-based approaches, such as metal NPs [12], quantum dots [13], carbon dots [14], and MOFs [10,15,16].

Metal nanoclusters comprised of a few to hundreds of atoms are becoming increasingly prevalent nowadays. Theyare atomically crystalline materials, most often existing on the 1–3 nm scale. These nanoparticles are commonly referred to as “metal nanoclusters” (MNCs) to distinguish them from typical plasmonic nanoparticles. They typically do not exhibit the conventional surface plasmon resonance absorption in the visible range, but rather fluorescence [15,17]. As a result, these MNCs are considered to have molecule-like properties and no longer have plasmonic resonance, which is referred to as bridge linkages between atoms and nanoparticles [17]. Furthermore, as compared to other fluorescent materials such as hazardous quantum dots and organic dyes, MNCs are simple to prepare and have minimal toxicity [18–20]. The fundamental disadvantage of MNCs is that they strongly interact with each other and aggregate permanently to minimize their surface energy, which can be overcome by utilizing stabilization [21,22]. The use of biomolecules as protective agents is a step towards the development of green chemistry approaches for the synthesis of MNCs. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) has been used as a capping agent for the synthesis of MNCs for a long time because of its vast research and strong affinity for inorganic ions [23].

Cysteine (Cys), as one of the biothiol, is a biomarker associated with various serious disorders, including cancer, Parkinson’s disease, autoimmune deficiency syndrome, and Alzheimer's disease [24]. Late development, hair depigmentation, oedema, liver damage, skin issues, and weakness are all symptoms of cyst insufficiency [25]. Cys is a typical biothiolthat contains sulphydryl; the coordination of sulfhydryl and Ag atoms allowed nanosilver probes to detect Cys.

The majority of ratiometric AgNCs-based probes are predominantly composed of organic dyes, and carbon dot [5,26–29]. These probes are vulnerable to irreversible photobleaching and have wide emission spectra with limited excitation bandwidths, leading to low-efficiency fluorescence detection. To this end, it is important to choose a materialto serve as a reference signal throughout the sensing process, whereas AgNCs exhibit a response toward Cys. Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have numerous inherent advantages as a unique fluorescent nanomaterial, including abundant raw material, less poisonousness, simplicity of synthesis, anti-photobleaching capabilities, and high stability [30,31]. These were the motivations for our attempt to integrate CuNCs with AgNCs to produce a ratiometric probe that could also function as a ratiometric fluorescence probe. Several studies on the detection of Cys using ratiometric nanoprobes have been published in the literature [1,13,30,32–35].

The naked eye’s color recognition is still insufficient for accurate and precise quantification [36]. Color visualization detection may be performed by reading RGB data using a color recognizer application on a smartphone, which corresponds to the growing trend of convenience and miniaturization of analytical instruments [36–38]. Recently, the analytical community has begun to explore a new option: using smartphones to detect various analytes as a smart detector. New smartphone cameras have shown to be the best analytical tools for digitizing photographs and displaying data since they are the most widely used and simple-to-use scanners with remarkable sensitivity to light and tonal variations.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no published study employing ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes integrated with a smartphone and RGB analysis for fluorescent Cys detection using two nanoclusters. It is crucial to focus on creating a unique ratiometric approach for detecting Cys using Cu@Ag NCs based nanoprobe combined with a smartphone and RBG analysis to address this gap.

Herein, to minimize the impact of experimental variables, a novel ratiometric fluorescent copper-silver nanocluster (Cu@Ag NCs) was proposed to detect biothiol (cysteine as an example, Cys) based on CuNCs as a reference signal and AgNCs as a response signal. The fluorescence intensity of AgNCs decreased with the addition of Cys. While CuNCs' fluorescence recovered and their color changed from red to blue. The relationship between the red/blue value intensity ratio and the Cys concentration was then quantified. Importantly, the stability of AgNCs was improved by utilizing uracil and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as complexing and stabilizing agents. Additionally, the nanoprobe was used with a smartphone-assisted RGB model based on quantitative biothiol analysis as well as semi-quantitative biothiol detection using only naked eye detection. Scheme 1 illustrates the roadmap for the fabrication of Cu@Ag NCs composites and Cys detection.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), silver nitrate (AgNO3), copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 10,000), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and the cations such as Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Fe(NO3)2, NaNO3, NH4Cl, and ZnCl2 were acquired from Merck and used as received. Uracil and the interference molecules such as ascorbic acid, citric acid, creatinine, d-mannose, folic acid, glucose, urea, thiourea, uric acid, and amino acids such as alanine, proline, histidine, lysine HCl, l-arginine, l-tyrosine, theobromine, cysteine, and glutathione were obtained from Carl Roth, Germany.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of CuNCs, AgNCs, and Cu@Ag NCs were recorded by a dual-beam spectrophotometer (PG Instrument T80 double beam, Great Britain). The photoluminescence spectra of the nanoprobes were recorded with a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF 5301, Japan) with a 450 W xenon arc lamp as the excitation source and a slit width of 10 mm. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology and particle size were assessed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a Zeiss-EM10C (100 KV, German). Zeiss sigma 300 was used to perform element mapping and energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS). A Hanna pH meter was used to determine the pH of the solutions in various buffer solutions. The RGB values of the fluorescent color images were recorded using the color grab app on a smartphone (Samsung Galaxy A7, 2018).

The BSA–CuNCswere synthesized using the prior approach with minor modifications [39,40]. BSA was used as a capping agent as well as a stabilizing agent. In a typical synthesis, 50 mg of BSA were dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water. After the BSA has been completely dissolved, 1.0 mL, 10 mM Cu(NO3)2·3H2O added and stirred for 5 min, the solution gradually became turbid. 1 M NaOH solution was added until the solution turned a clear purple. Finally, 300 μL of NaOH (1 M) and 120 μL of NaBH4 (10 mM) were loaded slowly. The reaction system was left at 55 °C for 2 h. The formation of CuNCs, which emit blue fluorescence when viewed under UV light, enabled the solution's color to shift from purple to light yellow. Then, the solution was dialyzed (at 500 MWCO) extensively against deionized waterfor 24 hto remove unreacted materials, and the resulting solution was kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

AgNCs were produced using previously published work in the literature with modifications [23]. Briefly, 250 mg of BSA, 2.5 mg of PVP, and 2.5 mg of uracil were dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water. Vigorous stirring was performed at room temperature while adding 5 mL of a 10 mM silver nitrate solution. Approximately 0.3 mL of NaOH (1 M) and 120 μL of NaBH4 (10 mM) solution were added drop-by-drop, and the solution turned from colorless to reddish brown, suggesting the formation of AgNCs, which show red fluorescence when viewed under UV light. The solution was dialyzed extensively against deionized waterfor 24 hto remove unreacted materials, and the resulting solution was kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

The previously prepared and purified CuNCs and AgNCs were thoroughly mixed at a volume ratio of 2:3, which was found to be the optimum ratio for the work and produced a red fluorescence when excited by UV light. The Cu@Ag NCs nanoprobe was obtained and kept at 4 °C.

In deionized water, a stock solution of cysteine (1 mM) was prepared. 1 mL of Cu@Ag NCs solution (2:3 vol ratio) was mixed with varying amounts of Cys stock solution (0 to 200 μM). The mixtures were diluted into 2 mL using deionized water as a diluent and vortexed well. The solutions were stimulated under UV light in a cabinet after 5 min of room temperature incubation, and a smartphone was used to capture images. Utilizing the Color Grab app as a free mobile app, the RGB values of the captured images were obtained, and a linear calibration curve was constructed by plotting the red-to-blue ratio of standard solutions and the concentration of Cys.

Several different interferents (common interferents in human serum) were systematically investigated to assess the selectivity of the sensing platform for Cys detection. The interferences include ascorbic acid, alanine, citric acid, urea, creatinine, folic acid, glucose, histidine, l-arginine, l-tyrosine, theobromine, uric acid, Fe(III), Fe(II) Zn(II), and Cu(II). All interfering concentrations were kept constant and were 10-fold higher than those of Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Healthly people's blood was collected at the Life Hospital in Kalar-Kurdistan, Iraq. Following earlier ethics committee approval for the analysis of Cys in human serum (GRCEC109). The blood samples were centrifuged at 5000 RPM for 5 min, and the supernatant serum was then collectedin a clean tube, and immediately stored at 4 °C for future investigations. 200 μL of serum samples were mixed with 1 mL of the ratiometric nanoprobe (Cu@Ag NCs) and spiked with varying concentrations of a standard Cys solution before being diluted into 2 mL. Following a 5 min incubation period at room temperature, the sample mixes were excited by UV light in a cabinet, and a smartphone was used to shoot digital pictures of them.

The images were captured at a 90-degree angle, 10 cm away from the samples, in a dark cabinet with a UV light. The 24-megapixel main sensor Galaxy Samsung A7 camera on a smartphone was utilized to shoot all digital photographs without the use of a flash. Using the smartphone app Color Grab, the RGB values of each fluorescent color image were acquired.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CuNCs, AgNCs and Cu@Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the morphology of the Cu@Ag NCs, TEM images were taken. These images showed that the Cu@Ag NCs had spherical particles that were evenly dispersed. Fig. 1 A reveals the TEM image of the Cu@Ag NCs, revealing that the nanocluster has a spherical-like shape with an average diameter of about 2.5 nm.

Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is one of the most extensively utilized methods for studying protein conformational changes and, in particular, secondary structure. The secondary structure of the protein is associated with the amide I band at ≈ 1643 cm−1 (mostly CO stretch) and the amide II band at ≈ 1528 cm−1 (CN stretch combined with NH bending mode). Because of transition dipole coupling, the amide I band is more sensitive to changes in protein secondary structure than amide II. Fig. 1B depicts the FTIR spectra of BSA-PVP-Uracil and Cu@Ag NCs. Many infrared peaks in the ligand can be seen in the Cu@Ag NCs when compared to the two infrared spectral lines. It is conceivable to suppose that the ligand efficiently binds to copper and silver. The stretching vibration peak of the –SH bond in BSA-PVP-Uracil is approximately 2350 cm−1. When Cu and Ag are coordinated, the –SH bond is destroyed and new bonds are formed, as indicated by the peak in Cu@Ag NCs being significantly weaker and almost disappearing. When the protein was bound to an Ag ion, the asymmetric vibrational intensity of C–OH at 1242 cm−1 also vanished due to coordination between Ag(I) and carboxyl groups. Moreover, a decrease in peak intensity reflects a decrease in the number of helical structures [19,41].

UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to investigate the optical properties of the produced CuNCs, AgNCs, and Cu@Ag NCs. Fig. 1C reveals the UV–vis absorption spectra of CuNCs, AgNCs, and Cu@Ag NCs. It is obvious that the absence of a typical surface plasmon resonance peak for Cu and Ag nanoparticles in the 400–600 nm range (400–500 nm for AgNPs and 500–600 for CuNPs) suggests that nanoclusters rather than larger nanoparticles formed. An absorption peak in the range of 240–250 nm is attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC bond [42]. CuNCs have the maximum fluorescence intensity when excited at 325 nm, whereas AgNCs have the highest fluorescence intensity when excited at 490 nm. When the two nanoclusters are combined, the maximal fluorescence peaks at 460 and 615 nm are observed when excited at 380 nm (Fig. 1D). The fluorescence spectra (the solid red line) clearly show a broad shoulder between 600 and 700 nm, which attributes to AgNCs and gives the solution red emission despite the presence of blue CuNCs.

The previously prepared and purified CuNCs and AgNCs were thoroughly mixed at a volume ratio of 2:3, which was found to be the optimum ratio for the work (Fig. S1). The pH of the solutions was adjusted to 12 during the synthesis of CuNCs and AgNCs by the addition of NaOH, as BSA is known to serve as a reducing agent as well as a capping agent in alkaline solutions. The synthesized AgNCs and CuNCs were evaluated in various buffer solutions and found to be working at physiological pH, which is the correct pH for working with biological samples (Fig. S2).

To investigate the chemical composition of AgNCs and CuNCs, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used (Fig. 2 A and B). In comparison to AgNCs and CuNCs (Fig. 2 A-D), the chemical composition and percentage change due to the use of different capping agents are shown. The elemental compositions of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, silver, and copper were convincingly confirmed by EDS mapping.

The addition of a small quantity of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) significantly increases the stability of the metal nanoclusters. The use of PVP, an organic surfactant, is beneficial for developing homogeneous metal nanoclusters with great dispersibility. Through weak coordination with metal ions, PVP is also known to increase the stability of AgNCs [23]. When compared to other nanoclusters, silver clusters are photosensitive, reactive, and readily aggregate. This problem is addressed by a small addition of uracil in addition to PVP, which enhances the stability of AgNCs. Silver and uracil can cooperate to form a variety of complexes, as can be seen in Fig. S3 [43].

A smartphone is useful for analyzing samples by capturing digital photographs, using color analysis tools such as Color Picker, Color Grab, and ImageJ, as well as linear analysis applications (regression),because of its effectiveness, accessibility, and broad applicability. By showing the color of the photographs, the smartphone can illustrate the linear correlation between color parameters and target content. One of the most popular color models was the RGB (red, green, and blue) model, which divided color into its three main components (Fig. 3 A).

The fluorescence quenching of BSA-AgNCs was caused by Cys' interaction with AgNCs. Cys has two potential interactions with AgNCs. The first kind of interaction is the replacement reaction, in which Cys, as an additional thiol ligand, might replace thiol ligands on the NC surface, destabilizing the NCs and leading to their aggregation. The second type of interaction is the etching of Ag atoms from the NC surface by thiol. This occurs because thiol groups interact with Ag atoms in a unique and effective way. The fluorescent AgNCs degrade into smaller AgNCs or even thiolate-Ag(I) complexes (Scheme 2 ), which do not emit fluorescence light. We hypothesized that the destruction of AgNCs by the thiol group in cysteine is the basic mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of AgNCs in our system, considering that cysteine and glutathione both quench AgNCs’ fluorescence in a comparable way.

AgNCs were fluorescently quenched by cysteine through complex formation between Ag and cysteine, with the rate of quenching proportional to cysteine concentration and CuNCs' blue fluorescence serving as a reference signal (Scheme 2). This hypothesis led to the suggestion of a ratiometric fluorescence method for the detection of cysteine, a typical biothiol. When the amount of cysteine was increased from 0 to 180 μM, Cu@Ag NCs nanoprobes showed a noticeable visual color shift. This shift may be semi-quantitatively seen with the naked eye when exposed to UV light. Images of fluorescent solutions were captured, and their values were collected using the Color Grab app to obtain the sample color information (RGB value). Fig. 3B demonstrates that the ratio of red to blue (red/blue) channels eventually changed as the concentration of cysteine increased. A well-defined linear relationship can be achieved between the red/blue values and cysteine concentrations from 0 μM to 180 μM. The detection limit was 7.0 μM and the correlation coefficient was 0.9968. The detection limit was calculated by LOD = KSo/S, in which K is the arithmetic factor chosen based on the confidence level anticipated. So is the standard deviation of the blank measurements (n = 12, K = 3), whereas S is the slope of the calibration curve [44].

The current study was compared with the smartphone-based detection methods used for cysteine detection, as shown in Table 1 . The linear range of the as-prepared Cu@Ag NCs is wider than some previously published ratiometrical works. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report for smartphone-based visual detection of cysteine using a dual-metallic nanocluster based on ratiometric fluorescence using RGB values.

To investigate the selectivity of cysteine detection as a typical biothiol, we evaluated most of the interferent chemicals instead of biothiol, such as ascorbic acid (a), citric acid (b), creatinine (c), l-tyrosine (d), folic acid (e), glucose (f), uric acid (g), histidine (h), alanine (i), l-arginine (j), urea (k), theobromine (l), Zn(II) (m), Cu(II) (n), Fe(III) (o), and Fe(II) (p). The results show that the fluorescence intensity was not reduced as it was in biothiol (Fig. 3C). Therefore, the developed Cu@Ag NCs ratiometric nanoprobe detection method has good selectivity for detecting cysteine or biothiol in general.

To test the viability of this suggested assay in a complex biological matrix, the amount of biothiol in human serum samples from a healthy individual was quantified with the aid of a ratiometric fluorescent Cu@Ag NCs probe. The diluted samples were spiked with different concentrations of cysteine, and pictures of solutions before and after spiking were recorded. Their red to blue values were determined using Color Grab apps, and the findings are described in Table 2 .
As can be seen, the spike recoveries ranged from 89 to 95 %, and the RSD% ranged from 2.5 to 3.4 %. The results confirm that our probe showed satisfactory accuracy and precision.

A dual-emission ratiometric sensor based on dual nanoclusters of Cu and Ag has been constructed and used to detect biothiols (cysteine as a typical biothiol). Blue CuNCs were used as a reference signal and red emissive AgNCs as a response signal. When compared to conventional single-emission techniques, the proposed approach revealed superior analytical performance. Addition of uracil greatly enhanced the stability of AgNC. The color of Cu@Ag NCs dual-cluster shifted from red to blue via the addition of cysteine. Importantly, a smartphone was able to record the visible color tonality of a bimetallic nanocluster probe under a 365 nm UV light, and with the use of a smartphone app (Color Grab), RGB analysis was used to quantify colors. This allowed us to establish a biothiol assay and detect it using a smartphone that had a good linear range and detection limit. The proposed method was simple, fast, selective, stable, and relevant to biothiol in real samples. Additionally, it confirmed using naked-eye visual assay for semi-quantitative detection of biothiols.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: For all of the assistance offered, the authors are grateful to the Universities of Sulaimani and Garmian.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108385.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel raiometric fluorescence probe based on silicon quantum dots and copper nanoclusters for visual assay of L-cysteine in milks
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma et al., “A novel raiometric fluorescence probe based on silicon quantum dots and copper nanoclusters for visual assay of L-cysteine in milks,” Food Chem., vol. 379, no. January, p. 132155, 2022, doi: 10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.132155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescence and visual imaging detection of dopamine based on carbon dots/copper nanoclusters dual-emitting nanohybrids
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. He, R. Gui, H. Jin, B. Wang, X. Bu, and Y. Fu, “Ratiometric fluorescence and visual imaging detection of dopamine based on carbon dots/copper nanoclusters dual-emitting nanohybrids,” Talanta, vol. 178, pp. 109–115, Feb. 2018, doi: 10.1016/j.talanta.2017.09.019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Specific enzymatic synthesis of 2,3-diaminophenazine and copper nanoclusters used for dual-emission ratiometric and naked-eye visual fluorescence sensing of choline
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Bu, Y. Fu, H. Jin, and R. Gui, “Specific enzymatic synthesis of 2,3-diaminophenazine and copper nanoclusters used for dual-emission ratiometric and naked-eye visual fluorescence sensing of choline,” New J. Chem., vol. 42, no. 21, pp. 17323–17330, 2018, doi: 10.1039/C8NJ03927E.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective colorimetric sensing strategy for cysteine based on an indicator-displacement mechanism
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xue, L. Xiong, H. Peng, H. Rao, X. Liu, and X. Lu, “A selective colorimetric sensing strategy for cysteine based on an indicator-displacement mechanism,” New J. Chem., vol. 42, no. 6, pp. 4324–4330, 2018, doi: 10.1039/C7NJ03887A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “A ratiometric nanoprobe based on silver nanoclusters and carbon dots for the fluorescent detection of biothiols”, Spectrochim
Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, B. Lin, Y. Yu, Y. Cao, M. Guo, and L. Shui, “A ratiometric nanoprobe based on silver nanoclusters and carbon dots for the fluorescent detection of biothiols,” Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc., vol. 195, pp. 230–235, Apr. 2018, doi: 10.1016/j.saa.2018.01.078.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for visual detection: Design principles and recent advances - a review
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Bigdeli et al., “Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for visual detection: Design principles and recent advances - A review,” Anal. Chim. Acta, vol. 1079, pp. 30–58, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.aca.2019.06.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Melamine-Induced Decomposition and Anti-FRET Effect from a Self-Assembled Complex of Rhodamine 6G and DNA-Stabilized Silver Nanoclusters Used for Dual-Emitting Ratiometric and Naked-Eye-Visible Fluorescence Detection
J. Agric. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fu, H. Jin, X. Bu, and R.
